Why the Right Cat Litter Tray Matters

A clean, well-chosen cat litter tray is one of the most important investments you can make for your cat’s health and your home’s hygiene. Cats are naturally fastidious animals that instinctively bury their waste, and providing them with a suitable tray encourages proper toileting habits, reduces stress, and prevents unwanted accidents around the home. The wrong tray — whether too small, too difficult to access, or poorly maintained — can lead to litter avoidance, behavioural problems, and even urinary health issues.

Whether you have a single indoor cat, a busy multi-cat household, or a new kitten, this guide will help you find cat litter trays that are hygienic, practical, and perfectly suited to your cat’s size, habits, and preferences.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat litter trays and shop by tray type to find the ideal match for your home.

Top 5 Best Cat Litter Trays

These are our top picks for clean, hygienic, and easy-to-maintain cat litter trays for every home.

Hooded Cat Litter Tray →

An enclosed litter tray with a carbon filter that traps odours and gives your cat complete privacy.

  • Hooded design — contains mess and gives cats a private, den-like space
  • Activated carbon filter — neutralises odours at the source
  • Swing door entry — easy access while keeping litter scatter contained
  • Large capacity — suitable for cats of all sizes including large breeds
  • Easy-clean smooth interior — wipe down in seconds for quick maintenance
View on Amazon →

Self-Cleaning Cat Litter Box →

An automatic self-cleaning litter box that scoops and separates waste after each use.

  • Automatic raking system — scoops waste into a sealed drawer hands-free
  • Reduces daily scooping — only empty the waste drawer once a week
  • Odour-lock receptacle — keeps smells sealed and contained
  • Quiet motor — won’t startle even the most nervous cats
  • Compatible with clumping litter — standard supermarket brands work perfectly
View on Amazon →

Large Cat Litter Tray with High Sides →

A spacious open-top tray with extra-high sides to prevent litter scatter and spillage.

  • Extra-high sides — prevents litter being kicked out during digging
  • Generous dimensions — comfortable for large breeds like Maine Coons
  • Non-stick surface — clumps slide off for effortless cleaning
  • Low-cut entry point — accessible for kittens and senior cats
  • Durable BPA-free plastic — built to last with daily use
View on Amazon →

Corner Cat Litter Tray →

A space-saving triangular tray designed to fit neatly into any corner of your home.

  • Corner-fit design — maximises floor space in small flats and rooms
  • High back walls — prevents splashing and litter scatter
  • Open front — easy access for cats of all ages and mobility levels
  • Lightweight construction — simple to lift, empty, and wash
  • Discreet placement — tucks away neatly out of sight
View on Amazon →

Cat Litter Tray with Sifting System →

A multi-layer sifting tray that separates clean litter from clumps without scooping.

  • Three-part sifting system — lift, sift, and separate waste in seconds
  • No scooping needed — saves time on daily litter maintenance
  • Works with clumping litter — compatible with all major clumping brands
  • Deep tray base — holds plenty of litter for extended use
  • Easy to disassemble — simple to wash and reassemble
View on Amazon →

Shop by Cat Litter Tray Type

Find the right cat litter tray based on your home size, cleaning preference, and your cat’s personality.

Open Litter Trays

Best for easy access and ventilation. Simple, uncovered trays that are affordable, easy to clean, and well-suited for kittens, anxious cats, or those who prefer open sightlines while toileting.

Hooded & Enclosed Trays

Ideal for odour control and privacy. Covered designs with carbon filters trap smells and give cats a secure, den-like space — perfect for busy households with children or other pets.

Self-Cleaning Trays

Great for busy owners who want hands-free maintenance. Automated raking or rotating systems sift waste into sealed compartments, reducing daily scooping to a simple weekly empty.

Sifting & Corner Trays

Perfect for small spaces and quick litter changes. Sifting trays separate clean litter from clumps in seconds, while corner designs maximise floor space in compact homes.

How to Choose the Right Cat Litter Tray

Choosing the right cat litter tray comes down to your cat’s size, your home layout, cleaning preferences, and any specific health or behavioural needs. A well-chosen tray promotes consistent use and keeps your home fresh.

📏 Choose the Right Size

The tray should be at least 1.5 times the length of your cat from nose to base of tail. A tray that is too small will be uncomfortable and may cause your cat to eliminate outside the box.

  • Standard adult cats need a tray measuring at least 50 cm x 40 cm.
  • Large breeds like Maine Coons need extra-large trays — 60 cm x 45 cm or bigger.
  • Kittens can start with a smaller, low-sided tray and transition to full-size by 6 months.
  • In multi-cat homes, provide one tray per cat plus one extra to prevent resource guarding.

🏠 Open vs Hooded Design

The choice between open and hooded trays depends on your cat’s personality and your household environment. Each design has distinct advantages.

  • Open trays: Better ventilation, easier to clean, preferred by anxious cats who need to see their surroundings.
  • Hooded trays: Contain odour and scatter, offer privacy, ideal for busy homes with children or dogs.
  • Top-entry trays: Excellent scatter control, dog-proof, but not suitable for senior or mobility-impaired cats.
  • If unsure, start with an open tray — most cats accept them readily.

🧹 Consider Ease of Cleaning

Regular cleaning is essential for your cat’s health. Choose a tray that fits your lifestyle and cleaning routine.

  • Choose trays with smooth, non-stick interiors that wipe clean easily.
  • Sifting trays save time — lift, sift, and separate waste in seconds.
  • Self-cleaning models are ideal if you struggle to scoop daily.
  • Replace plastic trays every 6–12 months as scratches harbour bacteria and odour.
  • Scoop at least twice daily — ammonia and hydrogen sulphide off-gas almost immediately, so frequent cleaning is the most effective odour control strategy.
  • Use activated carbon or charcoal additives to neutralise odours rather than masking them with heavily perfumed litters that can deter cats.
  • Weekly full clean: empty all used litter, wash the tray with warm water and mild unscented detergent, rinse thoroughly, and dry completely before adding fresh litter.
  • Monthly inspection: check for scratches, cracks, or damage that harbour bacteria — deep-clean with a pet-safe disinfectant and allow to air dry fully.
  • Maintain a litter depth of 5–7 cm and ensure good ventilation in the tray room to reduce lingering smells.
  • Monitor your cat’s litter habits — changes in frequency, unusual odour, or blood are early warning signs of urinary tract infections, kidney disease, or diabetes.

🐱 Think About Your Cat’s Preferences

Every cat has individual preferences around their toileting environment. Observing your cat’s behaviour is the best guide to choosing the right tray.

  • Cats that dig vigorously need high-sided trays to contain scatter.
  • Shy or nervous cats may prefer a hooded tray for privacy and security.
  • Senior cats need trays with a low-cut entry point for easy access.
  • If your cat suddenly stops using the tray, try a different style or location before assuming a medical issue.
  • Placement matters: keep trays away from food and water bowls — cats refuse to eat and toilet in the same area. Avoid high-traffic zones and spots near loud appliances.
  • In multi-storey homes, place at least one tray on each floor so your cat always has easy access.
  • For multi-cat households, spread trays across different rooms — a row of trays in one location is seen by cats as a single large tray, defeating the purpose of multiple options.
  • Quiet bathrooms, utility rooms, and spare bedrooms are consistently popular locations for cat litter trays.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right cat litter tray isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect solution based on their cat’s needs, home layout, and lifestyle.

🐱 New Kitten Owners

If you’re bringing home a kitten for the first time, this guide covers low-sided starter trays, litter training tips, and the best tray types for young cats still developing coordination and confidence.

🏠 Multi-Cat Households

Managing litter trays in a multi-cat home requires the right number, placement, and tray styles. We cover the n+1 rule, territorial considerations, and the best trays for homes with two or more cats.

🧓 Senior Cat Owners

Older cats with arthritis or reduced mobility need trays with low-cut entry points, easy-access designs, and generous dimensions. This guide highlights the best options for keeping senior cats comfortable.

Cat Litter Trays FAQ

How many cat litter trays do I need?

The general recommendation is one cat litter tray per cat, plus one extra. For a single cat household, having two trays is ideal. For two cats, three trays are recommended, and so on. This ensures every cat always has access to a clean tray and reduces the risk of resource guarding or territorial behaviour around the litter area.

How often should I replace cat litter trays?

Plastic trays should typically be replaced every six to twelve months, or sooner if they become heavily scratched. Scratches in the plastic create microscopic grooves where bacteria and odour-causing compounds can accumulate and cannot be fully cleaned away. Stainless steel cat litter trays last considerably longer and can be an economical long-term investment.

Why is my cat not using the litter tray?

There are many possible reasons a cat may avoid using a litter tray, including: the tray is not being cleaned frequently enough; the litter type has changed; the tray is in a stressful or noisy location; the tray is too small; a medical condition such as a urinary tract infection is causing pain; or stress from a change in the household. Always consult your vet first to rule out health issues, then work through environmental and litter-related factors systematically.

What depth of litter should I put in cat litter trays?

Most cats prefer a litter depth of approximately 5–7 cm. This gives them enough material to dig and cover their waste effectively, which is an important part of their natural behaviour. Too little litter and your cat may refuse to use the tray or waste will reach the bottom of the tray and stick. Too much litter can cause excessive scatter and may make some cats uncomfortable.

Should I use a liner in my cat litter trays?

Litter tray liners can make cleaning easier by allowing you to lift out all the used litter in one go, but they are not universally accepted by cats. Some cats dislike the feel or crinkle of plastic liners and may scratch or shred them, which defeats the purpose. If you want to try liners, introduce them gradually and if your cat rejects them, do not force the issue.

Can I use cat litter trays for kittens?

Yes, a standard tray is suitable for kittens, but choose a smaller option with low sides to start so the kitten can climb in and out easily. Very young kittens under 8 weeks may need extra-low sides. As the kitten grows, transition them gradually to a full-size tray. Avoid hooded designs for very young kittens as the enclosed space can be confusing and intimidating.

What is the best way to dispose of used cat litter?

In the UK, most used cat litter should be bagged securely and placed in your general waste bin — it cannot go in food waste or garden waste collections due to the risk of toxoplasmosis from cat faeces. Some biodegradable litters such as wood pellet or paper-based options can be composted in a dedicated compost bin. Never flush cat litter down the toilet, as it can damage plumbing and water treatment systems.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Beyond choosing the right tray, these practical guides will help you get the most out of your cat litter setup — from picking the best litter type to training a new kitten.

Complete Guide to Cat Litter Types

Choosing the right litter for your cat litter trays is just as important as choosing the tray itself. The four most common types available in the UK are clumping clay, silica gel crystals, wood pellets, and recycled paper.

  • Clumping clay litter — the most popular choice, forming solid clumps when wet for quick scooping and excellent odour control.
  • Silica gel crystals — absorb moisture and can last up to four weeks between full changes, making them economical despite a higher upfront cost.
  • Wood pellet litter — 100% biodegradable, made from compressed sawdust with a naturally pleasant pine scent. The most eco-friendly option.
  • Recycled paper litter — soft on paws and virtually dust-free, ideal for cats recovering from surgery or those with respiratory sensitivities.
  • Ensure your cat accepts the texture — some cats are particular about substrate and may reject an unfamiliar litter type. Transition gradually by mixing old and new litter over a week.

How to Litter Train a Kitten

Most kittens take to cat litter trays instinctively, but a gentle introduction helps establish good habits early. Here is a step-by-step approach to successful litter training.

  • Introduce early: place your kitten directly into the tray after meals, after naps, and any time you observe sniffing or circling behaviour.
  • Start with a low-sided tray so the kitten can climb in easily, and keep it in a consistent, accessible location away from food and water.
  • Never punish accidents — simply clean the area with an enzymatic cleaner and redirect them to their tray.
  • Kittens have small bladders and may need access to trays every 30 to 60 minutes during active periods.
  • Consistency and patience are the keys to success — most kittens are fully trained within one to two weeks of consistent use.
  • Avoid hooded designs for very young kittens as the enclosed space can be confusing and intimidating.

Travelling with Cats & Portable Litter Solutions

Whether you are moving house, visiting the vet, or going on a longer trip, having a portable litter solution ensures your cat remains comfortable and stress-free during travel.

  • Disposable travel trays — lightweight, pre-filled options perfect for short journeys and hotel stays.
  • Collapsible silicone trays — fold flat for easy packing and expand to full size when needed.
  • Bring litter from your cat’s existing tray — the familiar scent encourages use in an unfamiliar environment.
  • For long car journeys, set up a small tray in a secure carrier and offer breaks every two to three hours.
  • When moving house, confine your cat to one room initially with their familiar tray before gradually giving them access to the rest of the home.

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🛡️

Expert‑Reviewed Picks

Every cat litter tray we recommend is researched and evaluated for hygiene, durability, and ease of use.

⭐

Trusted by Pet Owners

Thousands of pet parents rely on our guides to make confident, informed buying decisions.

✔️

Independent & Honest

We only recommend products we believe in — no sponsored placements or paid rankings.

Explore More Cat Essentials

🛏️

Cat Beds

Give your cat the perfect place to rest with cosy caves, donut beds, orthopaedic memory foam, and self-warming options for all ages and breeds.

Shop All Cat Beds

Everything you need to keep your cat clean, healthy, and looking great — from brushes and nail clippers to shampoos and coat-care essentials.

Find safe, comfortable cat collars with bells, breakaway clips, and reflective designs for indoor and outdoor cats of all sizes.

Why Cats Need a Scratching Post

Scratching is one of the most fundamental behaviours in the feline world. It is not a sign of naughtiness or boredom — it is a deeply ingrained instinct that every cat needs to express. A quality cat scratching post gives your cat a dedicated outlet for claw maintenance, full-body stretching, territorial marking, and emotional regulation. Without one, cats will redirect this behaviour to your sofa, carpets, and door frames.

Whether your cat prefers to scratch vertically, horizontally, or at an angle, this guide will help you find cat scratching posts that are sturdy, satisfying, and perfectly suited to their size, scratching style, and living space.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat scratching posts and shop by type to find the ideal match for your feline friend.

Top 5 Best Cat Scratching Posts

These are our top picks for sturdy, satisfying, and durable cat scratching posts for all breeds and ages.

Tall Sisal Rope Scratching Post →

A sturdy full-height sisal post that lets cats stretch and scratch to their heart’s content.

  • Natural sisal rope — cats prefer it over carpet or furniture
  • Heavy weighted base — won’t topple even with vigorous scratching
  • 80cm tall — allows full-body stretching for adult cats
  • Replaceable sisal wrap — extend the life of the post
  • Small footprint — fits easily in any room
View on Amazon →

Cat Tree with Scratching Post →

A multi-level activity centre combining scratching posts, platforms, and a cosy hideaway.

  • Multiple scratching surfaces — sisal posts on every level
  • Plush platforms — perfect for resting and perching
  • Enclosed cave bed — private hideaway for shy cats
  • Dangling toy — built-in entertainment for playful cats
  • Sturdy construction — supports multiple cats at once
View on Amazon →

Shop by Scratching Post Type

Find the perfect scratching post for your cat based on their scratching style, your space, and your budget.

Sisal Rope Posts

Cat Trees & Towers

Cardboard Scratchers

Wall-Mounted Scratchers

Ideal for small spaces and modern homes. Mount directly to the wall to save floor space. Adjustable positioning and sleek designs that blend seamlessly with your decor.

How to Choose the Right Cat Scratching Post

Choosing the right cat scratching post comes down to your cat’s size, scratching style, the material they prefer, and where you place it. A well-chosen post protects your furniture and keeps your cat physically and mentally healthy.

📏 Consider Your Cat’s Size

The scratching post must be tall enough for your cat to stretch to full length. A post that’s too short will be ignored in favour of your furniture.

  • For most adult cats, choose a post at least 60–70 cm tall.
  • Large breeds like Maine Coons and Ragdolls need 80–100 cm or taller.
  • Kittens can start with smaller posts, but they’ll outgrow them quickly.
  • Always check the base width — it should be at least as wide as the post is tall for stability.

🪵 Choose the Right Material

Different cats have strong preferences for scratching surfaces. Offering the right material dramatically increases the chances your cat will use their post.

  • Sisal rope/fabric: The gold standard — coarse, durable, and universally loved by cats.
  • Corrugated cardboard: Affordable, satisfying texture, ideal for horizontal scratchers.
  • Carpet: Budget-friendly but can reinforce carpet-scratching habits elsewhere.
  • Natural wood/bark: Eco-friendly and instinctive — mimics scratching trees outdoors.

Sisal is a natural plant fibre with a coarse, rope-like texture that cats find enormously satisfying. Sisal-wrapped cat scratching posts fray in a way that mimics bark, appealing to cats’ instincts. A good sisal post can last two to four years with regular use.

Corrugated cardboard creates a deeply satisfying scratching experience and is the most affordable option. The downside is that it wears through quickly and sheds debris, so plan for regular replacements every one to four months.

Natural wood and bark — particularly birch logs — are increasingly popular for eco-conscious cat owners. Cats evolved to scratch trees, and many take to natural wood surfaces with great enthusiasm. They can be found at garden centres as well as pet shops.

🏠 Think About Placement

Even the best scratching post will be ignored if placed in the wrong spot. Cats scratch where it matters to them — usually in prominent, social areas.

  • Place posts near sleeping areas — cats scratch when they wake up.
  • Position in main living areas where your cat spends the most time.
  • If your cat scratches furniture, place the post right next to the targeted spot.
  • In multi-storey homes, provide at least one post per floor.

Small spaces need creative solutions. Wall-mounted scratchers are the ultimate space-saver — they fix directly to the wall and take up zero floor space. You can mount them at the precise height that suits your cat and combine them with wall shelves for a complete vertical playground.

Corner scratching posts fit snugly into room corners, protecting the exact spots cats tend to target while using minimal floor area. Door-hanging scratchers hook over any standard door with no permanent installation — ideal for renters who cannot drill into walls.

Even a simple flat cardboard scratcher pad can slide under furniture when not in use, providing an affordable, replaceable scratching option for the smallest of homes.

🐱 Match Your Cat’s Scratching Style

Observe how your cat scratches — their preferred orientation and surface will guide you to the best type of post.

  • Vertical scratchers (door frames, sofa sides) need tall upright sisal posts.
  • Horizontal scratchers (carpets, rugs) prefer flat cardboard pads or mats.
  • Angle scratchers benefit from inclined ramp-style scratchers.
  • Many cats enjoy multiple orientations — offer variety for the best results.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right cat scratching post isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect fit based on their cat’s needs, habits, and scratching style.

🐱 Kitten Owners

If you’re raising a kitten, establishing good scratching habits early is essential. This guide highlights the best starter posts and training tips to protect your furniture from day one.

🛋️ Furniture-Conscious Owners

If your cat is already scratching your sofa, carpets, or door frames, Pet Hub Online covers the most effective scratching posts and redirection techniques to save your home.

🐈 Multi-Cat Households

Managing scratching behaviour with multiple cats requires more posts, varied types, and strategic placement. This guide explains exactly how many you need and where to put them.

Cat Scratching Posts FAQ

How many cat scratching posts does one cat need?

Most cat behaviour experts recommend at least two cat scratching posts per cat: one vertical post in a main living area and one horizontal pad near a sleeping spot. If your home has multiple floors, each floor should have at least one cat scratching post. Having more cat scratching posts is rarely a problem — having too few almost always is.

How often should I replace a cat scratching post?

A sisal-wrapped cat scratching post used by one cat typically lasts one to three years before the covering is worn through. Cardboard horizontal pads may need replacing every one to four months depending on use. Check your cat scratching posts regularly — when the material is smooth, shredded to the core, or no longer engaging your cat, it’s time to replace it. Don’t wait until the post is completely destroyed, as a worn-out cat scratching post is less effective at claw maintenance.

My cat ignores the cat scratching post — what should I do?

First, check the location: a scratching post tucked in a corner is likely to be ignored. Move it to a prominent spot near where the cat sleeps or spends time. Second, check the height — if the post doesn’t allow a full stretch, try a taller one. Third, try a different material: if your post is carpet-covered, try sisal. If sisal isn’t working, try a cardboard pad. Finally, use catnip or silvervine to spark interest. Hanging a cat toy from the top of a vertical cat scratching post can also encourage a cat to reach up and start scratching.

Are cat scratching posts safe for kittens?

Yes — introducing cat scratching posts to kittens early is one of the best habits you can establish. Kittens start scratching at just a few weeks old, so having a small, stable cat scratching post available from the moment they arrive home helps build good habits from the start. Make sure the base is stable enough that a playful kitten can’t knock the post over — a toppled cat scratching post can frighten a kitten and put them off using it permanently.

Do declawed cats still need cat scratching posts?

Declawing is illegal in the UK under the Animal Welfare Act 2006, so this is less of a concern for UK cat owners. However, cats that have been declawed abroad still exhibit scratching behaviour — they go through the motions even without claws, as it’s an ingrained instinct. Declawed cats should still be provided with cat scratching posts to allow them to stretch, mark, and self-soothe, though softer materials like carpet or fleece are more comfortable for them than sisal.

Can I use cat scratching post deterrent spray on furniture?

Yes. Deterrent sprays use citrus or bitter scents that cats find unpleasant, making treated furniture less attractive as a scratching target. They work best when used alongside cat scratching posts placed nearby — deterring the furniture while simultaneously offering an appealing alternative. Always test sprays on a hidden patch of fabric first, as some formulations can affect upholstery colours. Also check out our cat grooming section for advice on regular nail trimming, which reduces the urgency of scratching and protects furniture.

What is the best cat scratching post for a large cat?

Large breeds need tall, heavy-duty cat scratching posts — look for a minimum height of 80–90 cm and a very wide, weighted base. Sisal-wrapped posts with a diameter of at least 10 cm are more satisfying for large cats than thin posts, which can feel unstable under their weight. Maine Coons, Ragdolls, and Norwegian Forest Cats in particular benefit from extra-tall cat scratching posts or full cat trees with robust scratching columns.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

From training your cat to use a scratching post to building your own DIY version, these practical tips will help you get the most out of your purchase.

🎓 How to Train Your Cat to Use a Scratching Post

Most cats will investigate a new cat scratching post fairly quickly, especially if it’s placed correctly and made from an appealing material. However, some cats — particularly those who have been scratching furniture for a long time — may need a little encouragement.

  • Step 1: Make it irresistible. Rub dried catnip into the surface or hang a favourite cat toy from the top. Placing the post near where the cat already spends time increases the likelihood of a first investigation.
  • Step 2: Reward any interaction. The moment your cat sniffs, rubs against, or scratches the post, reward them immediately with a treat and verbal praise. You’re building a positive association between the cat scratching post and good things happening.
  • Step 3: Redirect, don’t punish. If you catch your cat scratching the sofa, calmly carry them to the nearest cat scratching post. Gently place their paws on the surface. Never shout at or punish a cat for scratching — punishment creates anxiety, which often increases scratching behaviour.
  • Step 4: Make unwanted surfaces less appealing. Temporarily protect furniture with double-sided sticky tape, aluminium foil, or scratch-deterrent sprays. Most of these solutions are only needed for a few weeks while the cat learns to prefer their new posts.
  • Step 5: Be patient and consistent. Training can take anywhere from a few days to a few weeks. Consistency is key — every time you redirect your cat and reward them, you reinforce the behaviour. Most cats are fully redirected within a month.

🔨 DIY Cat Scratching Post Ideas

If you’re handy with basic tools, making your own cat scratching posts is a rewarding and cost-effective project. Homemade posts can be customised exactly to your cat’s size preferences and your home’s décor.

  • Simple sisal post: A piece of 4×4 timber (at least 70 cm tall), a heavy base board, wood screws, and a roll of sisal rope. Sand the timber smooth, fix it vertically to the base, then wrap tightly with sisal from bottom to top, securing each end with a staple gun. Cover the base with carpet to prevent sliding — a sturdy post for under £20.
  • Wall-mounted post: Fix fence posts to wall brackets for a space-saving vertical scratcher. Or fashion horizontal pads from offcuts of sisal matting stapled to a backing board.
  • Natural branch scratcher: Natural tree branches — particularly birch or apple wood — can be mounted at an angle to create a natural-looking cat scratching post that doubles as décor.
  • Easy repairs: The main advantage of DIY posts is repairability. When the sisal wears through, simply unwrap the old rope and rewrap with new. This extends the life of a well-built frame almost indefinitely, making it a sustainable and economical choice.

Why the Right Cat Collar Matters

A cat collar is far more than a simple accessory — it is one of the most important safety tools you can give your feline companion. Whether your cat roams the neighbourhood or lives entirely indoors, a properly fitted collar with visible identification dramatically increases the chance of a safe return if they ever go missing. In the UK, where microchipping is strongly recommended but not yet compulsory for cats, a collar with an ID tag offers instant visible contact information that anyone can read without a scanner.

Whether you need a breakaway safety collar, a reflective hi-vis band, or a GPS tracker, this guide will help you find cat collars that are safe, comfortable, and perfectly suited to your cat’s size, lifestyle, and personality.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat collars and shop by collar type to find the ideal match for your feline friend.

Shop by Cat Collar Type

Find the perfect collar, lead, or harness for your cat based on their lifestyle, safety needs, and your personal style preference.

Breakaway Collars

The safest option for all cats. Quick-release buckles snap open under pressure to prevent choking if the collar catches on a branch, fence, or furniture. Recommended by the RSPCA and Cats Protection.

Harnesses & Leads

Ideal for outdoor walks and adventures. Vest-style harnesses distribute pressure across the chest rather than the neck, giving you more control and preventing escape during supervised outdoor time.

Reflective & Hi-Vis Collars

Essential for outdoor and night-roaming cats. Reflective strips catch car headlights and torchlight, making your cat visible during UK winter evenings and early mornings.

Personalised & ID Collars

Perfect for identification without dangling tags. Engraved, embroidered, or printed collars display your contact details directly on the band for instant identification if your cat goes missing.

How to Choose the Right Cat Collar

Choosing the right cat collar depends on your cat’s lifestyle, size, and safety needs. A well-chosen collar keeps your cat identifiable, visible, and safe at all times.

Measure for the Right Fit

Getting the right fit is the single most important step when choosing a cat collar. A collar that is too tight restricts breathing and can cause painful skin abrasions, while one that is too loose can slip off or snag on objects. Start by wrapping a soft tape measure or piece of string around the base of your cat’s neck where the collar will sit naturally, then add the width of two fingers (approximately 2-3 cm). This two-finger rule is the standard recommendation from UK vets, the RSPCA, and Cats Protection.

  • Wrap a soft tape measure around the base of your cat’s neck where the collar will sit and note the measurement.
  • Add two fingers’ width of space (about 2-3 cm) — this is the standard RSPCA recommendation for safe clearance.
  • Kittens: 15-20 cm | Small adults: 20-28 cm | Standard adults: 25-35 cm | Large breeds (Maine Coons, Ragdolls): 30-40 cm.
  • Once fitted, check you can slide two flat fingers between the collar and neck. If you can’t, loosen it immediately; if more than two fit, tighten it to prevent jaw or paw entanglement.
  • Re-check the fit every 2-3 weeks for adult cats and every 5-7 days for fast-growing kittens.
  • After putting a new collar on, observe your cat for 30 minutes to ensure they are comfortable and not scratching excessively.

Choose a Breakaway Mechanism

A breakaway (quick-release) buckle is the single most important safety feature in any cat collar. It snaps open under pressure if your cat becomes caught on a branch, fence, or piece of furniture, preventing strangulation, degloving injuries, or broken limbs. Without a breakaway buckle, a trapped cat collar can cause life-threatening harm. Both the RSPCA and Cats Protection strongly recommend that all cat collars should have a breakaway mechanism.

  • Look for buckles that release under 4-6 kg of force — strong enough for play, safe enough to open if your cat is trapped.
  • The RSPCA and Cats Protection both recommend breakaway collars for all cats, especially those allowed outdoors.
  • Test the breakaway buckle monthly to ensure it still releases at the correct pressure — mechanisms can stiffen over time.
  • Never use a fixed-buckle collar on an unsupervised cat — fixed buckles have been linked to serious injuries including strangulation.
  • Check that collars are free from sharp edges, exposed metal, or toxic dyes — smooth, rounded hardware prevents skin irritation and fur snagging.
  • Many modern cat collars include an elasticated safety section as a secondary release mechanism in case the buckle does not open.
  • Replace cat collars every 6-12 months or sooner if you notice fraying, wear, or damage to the buckle mechanism.

Consider Reflective Features

If your cat goes outdoors at any time — especially during the darker months — a reflective collar can be a lifesaver by making them visible to drivers and pedestrians. Indoor and outdoor cat collars serve different purposes: indoor collars prioritise comfort with soft fabrics like cotton, velvet, or neoprene, while outdoor collars need waterproof nylon or biothane that withstands the UK’s wet weather. Ideally, use a lightweight indoor collar for everyday identification and a more robust reflective outdoor collar when your cat ventures outside.

  • Choose collars with 360-degree reflective strips for maximum visibility from any angle during UK winter evenings and early mornings.
  • Many reflective collars also include a breakaway buckle — the best of both worlds for safety and visibility.
  • Hi-vis neon colours (yellow, orange) offer daytime visibility too, making your cat easier to spot in gardens and hedgerows.
  • Pair with a bell to further protect local wildlife — wildlife conservationists recommend bells to warn birds and small mammals.
  • For indoor cats, prioritise soft, lightweight fabrics (cotton, velvet, neoprene) that are comfortable for all-day wear.
  • Outdoor cat collars should be waterproof or water-resistant to prevent moisture sitting against the skin in rainy UK weather.
  • For the best protection, consider adding a GPS tracker attachment to your outdoor cat’s collar for real-time location monitoring.

Add Identification

A collar with visible ID information is one of the fastest ways to reunite a lost cat with its owner. Even microchipped cats benefit from instant visual identification — not everyone who finds a stray cat has access to a microchip scanner. In the UK, where microchipping is strongly recommended but not yet compulsory for cats, a collar with an ID tag provides instant contact information that anyone can read.

  • Choose engraved buckles or embroidered text for tag-free, snag-free identification that won’t catch on objects.
  • Include at least one mobile phone number so you can be reached quickly if your cat is found.
  • QR code collars link to an online profile with full medical history, dietary needs, and multiple contact details.
  • Avoid putting your full home address on the collar for personal security reasons — a phone number is sufficient.
  • Personalised collars with printed or laser-engraved details are particularly popular for indoor cats as they avoid dangling tags.
  • A collar and a microchip complement each other perfectly — the collar provides instant visible ID while the microchip provides permanent, tamper-proof identification readable by any vet or rescue centre.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right cat collar isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect collar based on their cat’s lifestyle, safety needs, and daily routine.

🐱 Indoor Cat Owners

Even indoor cats can escape through open doors or windows. A lightweight breakaway collar with ID ensures your cat can be identified and returned quickly if they slip out.

🌳 Outdoor & Adventurous Cat Owners

If your cat roams freely or enjoys lead walks, this guide covers reflective collars, GPS trackers, and escape-proof harnesses designed to keep adventurous cats safe.

🐾 New Kitten Owners

First-time kitten owners will find advice on when to start wearing a collar, which kitten-sized options are safest, and how to introduce a collar gently without causing stress.

Cat Collars FAQ

Should all cats wear cat collars?

Most vets and animal welfare organisations recommend that cats wear collars, provided the collar has a safety-release buckle. Even microchipped cats benefit from wearing a cat collar with an ID tag, as it provides instant visible identification without the need for a scanner. Indoor cats, outdoor cats, and kittens can all safely wear appropriately sized collars.

At what age can kittens start wearing cat collars?

Most kittens can start wearing collars from around 6 months of age, once they’ve reached a size where the collar can be properly fitted without being too loose. Some lightweight kitten-specific collars are designed for cats as young as 3–4 months. Always check the manufacturer’s weight and age recommendations, and monitor the fit closely as kittens grow very quickly.

How tight should a cat collar be?

A cat collar should be snug enough to stay in place but loose enough to slip two fingers comfortably underneath. If you can’t fit two fingers under the collar, it’s too tight and could cause discomfort or injury. If you can fit more than two fingers, the collar is too loose and may catch on objects or slip over the cat’s head.

Are breakaway cat collars really safer?

Yes. Breakaway collars are overwhelmingly recommended by vets and animal welfare charities for cats that spend time outdoors. Fixed-buckle collars have been linked to serious injuries when cats become trapped, including broken limbs, degloving injuries, and strangulation. Breakaway collars release under sufficient pressure, allowing the cat to escape safely. The only scenario where a fixed buckle might be preferred is when walking a cat on a lead with a harness — in which case a harness, not a collar, should be used for lead attachment.

How often should I replace my cat’s collar?

Cat collars should be inspected regularly for signs of wear, fraying, or damage to the buckle mechanism. As a general rule, replace collars every 6–12 months, or sooner if you notice any deterioration. The breakaway buckle mechanism in particular should be tested periodically to ensure it still releases at the appropriate pressure. Check the fit every few weeks and adjust as needed, especially for kittens and cats that gain or lose weight.

Can cats wear collars with bells?

Yes, and many wildlife organisations actively encourage it. A bell attached to a cat collar gives birds and small mammals a warning of an approaching cat, helping to reduce predation. Most cats adapt quickly to the sound of a bell. If your cat seems distressed by the noise initially, you can start with a quieter bell and gradually move to a louder one. Browse cat collars with bells on Amazon UK for a variety of options.

Do cat collars interfere with microchips?

No. Cat collars do not interfere with microchips in any way. Microchips are implanted under the skin between the shoulder blades, well away from the neck where collars sit. A collar and a microchip complement each other perfectly — the collar provides instant visible identification, while the microchip provides permanent, tamper-proof identification that can be read by any vet or rescue centre in the UK.

Where can I buy cat collars in the UK?

Cat collars are widely available across the UK from pet shops, supermarkets, and online retailers. For the widest selection, competitive prices, and customer reviews, Amazon UK is an excellent starting point, with next-day delivery available on many collars. You can also explore our curated selection of cat supplies, including collars, beds, litter trays, and much more.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Beyond choosing the right collar, these practical tips will help you keep your cat safe, comfortable, and happy — whether they stay indoors or explore the great outdoors.

Cat Harness Training: A Step-by-Step Guide

Harness training a cat takes patience, but the reward is safe, enriching outdoor time for cats that might otherwise be confined indoors. The key is to take it slowly and let your cat set the pace. Most cats can be successfully harness-trained within 2-4 weeks if you follow a gradual introduction process.

  • Step 1 — Familiarisation: Leave the harness near your cat’s favourite resting spot for a few days so they can sniff and investigate it at their own pace.
  • Step 2 — Draping: Drape the harness loosely over your cat’s back during play or treat time without fastening it, building positive associations.
  • Step 3 — Indoor wear: Fasten the harness indoors for short periods (5-10 minutes), gradually increasing the duration over a week. Reward calm behaviour with treats.
  • Step 4 — Attach the lead: Once your cat walks comfortably indoors in the harness, attach the lead and let them drag it (supervised) around the house.
  • Step 5 — Guided indoor walks: Hold the lead loosely while following your cat around the house — never pull or drag them.
  • Step 6 — First outdoor session: Choose a quiet, enclosed garden and keep sessions short (10-15 minutes). Use treats to encourage movement.
  • Harness type: Choose a vest-style harness rather than a figure-eight design — vest harnesses are harder to escape from and distribute pressure more evenly across the chest.

Browse our top-rated cat harness and lead sets on Amazon UK to find the perfect fit for your feline adventurer. Pair with our recommended cat beds so your explorer always has a cosy spot to return to.

Seasonal Cat Collar Care Tips

Cat collars need different attention throughout the year. A little seasonal maintenance keeps your cat comfortable and their collar functioning properly in all weather conditions.

  • Spring: Check the fit after winter weight changes and replace any collars showing wear from the wet season. Flea collar season begins — ensure flea collars also have breakaway buckles.
  • Summer: Choose lightweight, breathable collar materials to prevent overheating. Light colours reflect heat better than dark ones. Ensure ID tags are legible — sun exposure can fade printed details.
  • Autumn: Switch to reflective or hi-vis collars as daylight hours shorten. UK clocks go back in October, meaning your cat may be outside in darkness during previously light hours.
  • Winter: Waterproof collars are essential during the UK’s wettest months. Check collars more frequently for stiffness caused by cold and damp, which can affect the breakaway mechanism.
  • Year-round: Wash fabric collars monthly in warm soapy water, rinse thoroughly, and air dry. Replace any collar that shows fraying, fading, or buckle stiffness regardless of age.

Why Cat Grooming Matters

Many cat owners assume that because cats self-groom, they don’t need any help. In reality, regular grooming is a shared responsibility that goes far beyond keeping your cat’s coat looking good. Proper grooming sessions strengthen the bond between you and your pet, allow you to check for lumps, skin irritation, fleas, and parasites, and help you detect early signs of health conditions before they become serious.

Whether your cat has a short, medium, or long coat, this guide will help you find the best cat grooming tools that are safe, effective, and perfectly suited to their breed, age, and grooming needs.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat grooming tools and shop by category to find the ideal products for your feline friend.

Top 5 Best Cat Grooming Tools

These are our top picks for essential cat grooming tools that make grooming easier and more enjoyable for both you and your cat.

Self-Cleaning Slicker Brush →

A gentle yet effective slicker brush that removes loose fur, prevents matting, and keeps your cat’s coat smooth and healthy.

  • Self-cleaning button — retract bristles for easy fur removal
  • Fine bent wire pins — gentle on skin, tough on tangles
  • Ergonomic handle — comfortable grip for longer grooming sessions
  • Suitable for all coat types — short, medium, and long hair
  • Reduces shedding by up to 90% — keeps your home fur-free
View on Amazon →

Professional Cat Nail Clippers →

Sharp, precise nail clippers designed specifically for cats with a built-in safety guard to prevent over-cutting.

  • Sharp stainless steel blades — clean cuts every time
  • Safety guard — prevents over-cutting and injury
  • Ergonomic non-slip handles — comfortable and secure grip
  • Suitable for all cat sizes — from kittens to large breeds
  • Includes nail file — smooth rough edges after trimming
View on Amazon →

Cat Grooming Glove →

A soft rubber grooming glove that removes loose fur while mimicking the sensation of being stroked — perfect for nervous cats.

  • 255 silicone tips — gentle massage while removing loose hair
  • Feels like petting — ideal for cats who dislike brushes
  • Adjustable wrist strap — fits all hand sizes securely
  • Works on wet or dry fur — versatile for bath time too
  • Easy to clean — peel fur off the glove in one motion
View on Amazon →

Cat Shampoo (Natural & Gentle) →

A pH-balanced, natural cat shampoo formulated with gentle ingredients that clean without stripping essential oils from your cat’s coat.

  • pH-balanced for cats — won’t irritate sensitive skin
  • Natural ingredients — aloe vera, oatmeal, and chamomile
  • No harsh chemicals — free from parabens, sulphates, and dyes
  • Leaves coat soft and shiny — conditions while it cleans
  • Pleasant natural scent — not overpowering for your cat
View on Amazon →

Cat Dematting Comb →

A professional-grade dematting tool with rounded blade edges that safely cut through mats and tangles without pulling or hurting your cat.

  • Dual-sided design — 9 teeth for stubborn mats, 17 teeth for thinning
  • Rounded blade edges — cut mats without scratching skin
  • Stainless steel blades — durable and rust-resistant
  • Non-slip rubber handle — secure grip during use
  • Ideal for long-haired breeds — Persians, Maine Coons, Ragdolls
View on Amazon →

Shop by Cat Grooming Category

Find the right grooming tools for your cat based on their specific needs, from daily brushing to bath time essentials.

Brushes & Combs

The foundation of every grooming routine. Slicker brushes, deshedding tools, and fine-tooth combs keep your cat’s coat tangle-free, reduce shedding, and distribute natural oils for a healthy shine.

Nail Care

Essential for indoor cats especially. Professional-grade nail clippers, grinders, and nail caps help keep claws at a safe, comfortable length and protect your furniture from scratching damage.

Shampoos & Bathing

For occasional deep cleans. pH-balanced cat shampoos, waterless foam cleansers, and grooming wipes keep your cat’s coat fresh and clean without stripping their natural oils or causing skin irritation.

Ear & Eye Care

Often overlooked but vital. Gentle ear cleaning solutions, eye wipes, and tear stain removers help prevent infections and keep your cat comfortable, especially for flat-faced breeds like Persians.

How to Choose the Right Cat Grooming Tools

Choosing the right grooming tools depends on your cat’s coat type, temperament, and specific grooming needs. The right tools make grooming easier, safer, and more enjoyable for both you and your cat.

🐱

Consider Your Cat’s Coat Type

Your cat’s coat type is the single biggest factor in choosing grooming tools. Short-haired cats need different tools than long-haired breeds.

  • Short-haired cats — a rubber grooming glove or soft bristle brush is usually sufficient for weekly sessions.
  • Medium-haired cats — a slicker brush combined with a fine-tooth comb handles tangles effectively.
  • Long-haired breeds — invest in a dematting comb, slicker brush, and wide-tooth comb for daily grooming.
  • Double-coated breeds — an undercoat rake or deshedding tool is essential during moulting season.
  • Brush in the direction of hair growth — use short, gentle strokes; hold fur between tangles and skin to prevent pulling.
  • Never cut out mats with scissors — feline skin is paper-thin; use a dematting comb or seek professional grooming help.
  • Pay extra attention to problem areas — behind the ears, under the armpits, and around the collar are prime spots for tangles.
  • Increase frequency during moulting season — spring and autumn shedding may require twice-daily sessions for long-haired breeds.

✂️

Start with the Basics

You don’t need to buy everything at once. Start with a core set of tools and expand as you learn what works best for your cat.

  • A good brush or comb — the most essential tool; choose based on coat type.
  • Nail clippers — every cat owner needs a pair; opt for ones with a safety guard.
  • Grooming wipes — perfect for quick clean-ups between full grooming sessions.
  • Treats — always have rewards on hand to build positive grooming associations.
  • Ear cleaning supplies — a damp cotton ball for inspecting ears each session; never insert anything into the ear canal.
  • Eye care cloths — a soft damp cloth for wiping discharge from inner corners; flat-faced breeds need daily cleaning.

🛁

Choose Gentle Products

Cats have sensitive skin, and many products designed for dogs or humans can be harmful. Always choose cat-specific grooming products.

  • Never use dog shampoo on cats — many contain ingredients toxic to felines.
  • Look for natural ingredients — oatmeal, aloe vera, and chamomile are safe and soothing.
  • Avoid strong fragrances — cats have a much more sensitive sense of smell than humans.
  • Check for pH balance — cat skin is different from human skin; use pH-appropriate products.
  • Waterless shampoos are excellent alternatives — ideal for cats that refuse baths, allowing coat cleaning without water stress.
  • Use lukewarm water only for baths — water comfortable on the inside of your wrist is the right temperature for your cat.
  • Rinse shampoo thoroughly — residue left in the coat causes skin irritation and dullness; rinse twice if necessary.
  • Prepare everything before bathing — gather towels, shampoo, a non-slip mat, and cotton wool for ears before you start.

🗥

Don’t Forget Nail & Dental Care

A complete grooming routine includes more than just coat care. Nail trimming and dental hygiene are equally important for your cat’s health.

  • Trim nails every 2–3 weeks — prevents painful overgrowth and furniture damage.
  • Use cat-specific toothpaste — human toothpaste contains fluoride, which is toxic to cats.
  • Start dental care early — kittens introduced to tooth brushing adapt much more easily.
  • Dental treats supplement brushing — but should not replace it entirely.
  • Identify the quick before cutting — the pink blood vessel inside the nail; cut only the sharp curved tip below it.
  • Keep styptic powder on hand — if you accidentally cut the quick, apply styptic powder or cornstarch to stop bleeding.
  • Don’t forget the dewclaws — the thumb-equivalent claw on each front paw never wears down and is most likely to overgrow.
  • Trim just a few nails per session if needed — if your cat becomes stressed, come back to the remaining nails later.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right grooming tools isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect products based on their cat’s breed, coat type, and grooming needs.

🐱 Long-Haired Cat Owners

If your cat has a long or thick coat, this guide highlights the best brushes, dematting combs, and deshedding tools to keep their fur tangle-free and healthy.

🐾 New Cat Owners

For first-time cat owners looking to build a complete grooming toolkit, this guide covers everything you need to get started with confidence and the right products.

🧓 Senior Cat Owners

If your cat is older and needs gentle grooming with extra care, we highlight the softest tools, easiest techniques, and products designed for sensitive, ageing cats.

Cat Grooming FAQ

How often should I groom my cat?

The frequency of cat grooming depends entirely on your cat’s coat type. Short-haired cats benefit from cat grooming once or twice a week. Medium-haired cats should have cat grooming sessions two to three times weekly. Long-haired breeds require daily cat grooming to prevent matting. Regardless of coat type, nail trimming is typically needed every two to three weeks, and ear checks should be part of every cat grooming session.

My cat hates being groomed — what can I do?

Resistance to cat grooming is extremely common, particularly in cats that weren’t introduced to handling from a young age. The key is to start small: begin with very brief cat grooming sessions of just a minute or two, paired with high-value treats and calm, gentle handling. Gradually increase the duration as your cat builds positive associations with cat grooming. Grooming gloves, which feel like stroking to many cats, can be an excellent bridge tool. If your cat becomes genuinely distressed or aggressive during cat grooming, consult your vet to rule out pain as a contributing factor.

Can I use dog grooming products on my cat?

No — never use dog grooming products on cats, particularly flea treatments and shampoos. Many ingredients safe for dogs are toxic to cats, and using dog-formulated products as part of your cat grooming routine can cause serious illness or death. Always use products specifically labelled as safe for cats, and check with your vet if you’re unsure about any cat grooming product.

Should I take my cat to a professional groomer?

Professional cat grooming is a valuable option for certain situations: severely matted coats, cats that cannot be handled safely at home for grooming, long-haired cats whose owners lack the time for daily cat grooming, or cats requiring specialist services like lion cuts. Professional cat groomers in the UK are trained to handle cats safely and have access to specialist equipment that makes the cat grooming process more efficient. Look for groomers who specifically advertise feline cat grooming experience, as not all dog groomers are equipped or trained to handle cats.

How do I stop my cat scratching furniture during grooming?

Scratching furniture is separate from cat grooming, but both are related to nail health. Regular nail trimming as part of your cat grooming routine reduces the damage caused by scratching, while providing appropriate cat scratching posts gives your cat a designated outlet for this natural behaviour. Nail caps (soft plastic covers glued over the nail) are another option used by some cat owners as a complement to cat grooming, though they require regular replacement as nails grow.

What’s the best cat grooming brush for shedding?

For managing shedding, a deshedding tool designed for cats — such as those modelled on the Furminator design — is the most effective option for your cat grooming toolkit. These tools reach into the undercoat to remove loose fur at the source, dramatically reducing the amount shed onto furniture and clothing. For daily cat grooming maintenance between deshedding sessions, a soft slicker brush or rubber grooming glove works well for all coat types. You can browse cat deshedding brushes on Amazon UK to find the right fit for your cat’s coat.

At what age should I start grooming my kitten?

The earlier you introduce cat grooming, the better. Kittens as young as two to three weeks old are groomed by their mothers, so they have an instinctive familiarity with the sensation. Begin gentle cat grooming handling as soon as your kitten arrives home, using a very soft brush and keeping sessions extremely short and positive. Introducing nail trimming and ear checks as part of kitten cat grooming means your cat will be accustomed to these procedures as an adult, making lifelong cat grooming maintenance significantly easier.

Useful Tips for Every Pet Owner

Whether you are a first-time cat owner or have years of experience, these practical tips will help you keep grooming sessions safe, efficient, and enjoyable for both you and your feline companion.

  • Groom at the right time — choose a moment when your cat is relaxed or sleepy; after a meal or play session is ideal.
  • Keep sessions short at first — start with one to two minutes and gradually build up as your cat gains confidence.
  • Place a non-slip mat on your grooming surface — whether bathing or brushing, a stable surface helps your cat feel secure.
  • Wrap your cat in a towel for nail trims — a loose burrito wrap with one paw exposed at a time makes trimming easier and calmer.
  • Check ears during every grooming session — healthy ears are pale pink inside with no debris, redness, or unpleasant odour.
  • Brush your cat’s teeth two to three times per week — use cat-specific enzymatic toothpaste; dental gels and water additives can supplement between sessions.
  • Bathe your cat only when truly necessary — most cats need a bath just a few times a year; exceptions include skin conditions, show cats, and hairless breeds like the Sphynx.
  • Blot dry rather than rubbing — wrap your cat in a warm towel and gently blot excess water to avoid stressing the coat and skin.
  • Use grooming as a health check — run your hands through your cat’s coat to detect lumps, bumps, weight changes, or skin abnormalities early.
  • Invest in a deshedding tool during moulting season — tools such as the Furminator reach into the undercoat to remove loose fur at the source, dramatically reducing shedding on furniture.
  • Always reward after grooming — high-value treats after every session build strong positive associations over time.
  • Schedule annual dental check-ups with your vet — professional dental cleanings complement your at-home routine and catch issues early.

Explore our full range of cat supplies to find everything you need for a complete grooming routine. You can also browse cat grooming brushes on Amazon UK alongside our guides on cat toys, cat beds, and cat scratching posts to keep every aspect of your cat’s life covered.

Start Your Cat Grooming Journey Today

Establishing a consistent cat grooming routine is one of the most valuable things you can do for your cat’s health, comfort, and wellbeing. Whether you’re just starting out with a new kitten or looking to upgrade your grooming toolkit, the right tools make all the difference.

Shop Cat Grooming
View All Cat Supplies

Grooming Senior Cats: Special Considerations

As cats age, their grooming needs change significantly. Senior cats (typically aged eleven and above) often have reduced flexibility that makes self-grooming increasingly difficult, making your role as groomer more important than ever.

You may notice that your older cat’s coat begins to look less well-groomed, with areas of matting or greasiness at the base of the tail and along the spine — areas that are hardest for a stiff cat to reach. Arthritis, dental pain, and obesity can all limit your cat’s ability to groom these areas effectively. Increased grooming support from you helps compensate for this reduced self-grooming capacity and maintains your cat’s dignity and comfort.

Keep sessions short and frequent. A five-minute grooming session each day is far more manageable for an arthritic older cat than a thirty-minute session once a week. Use the softest brush options available — grooming gloves are particularly well-suited for senior cats, as they feel more like gentle stroking than brushing. Be especially careful around bony prominences, joints, and any areas where your cat shows signs of discomfort.

Senior cats need more frequent nail trimming. Older cats tend to be less active, meaning their nails don’t wear down through natural scratching and movement. Overgrown nails in senior cats can quickly become a serious welfare issue — they curl and grow into the paw pad, causing infection and chronic pain. Check your senior cat’s nails every week and trim as needed.

Use grooming as a health check. Running your hands through your cat’s coat during grooming allows you to monitor muscle mass and detect weight loss that often accompanies hyperthyroidism, kidney disease, diabetes, and cancer — conditions significantly more common in older cats. Report any lumps, bumps, skin changes, or weight changes to your vet promptly. A comfortable cat bed with good orthopaedic support and a low-entry litter tray complement your senior cat’s grooming routine beautifully.

Why the Right Cat Bed Matters

Cats sleep between 12 and 16 hours per day on average, which means your cat will spend more than half their life resting. Providing dedicated, comfortable cat beds isn’t merely a luxury — it’s a fundamental part of responsible cat ownership. A quality bed gives your cat a sense of security, a defined territory within the home, and a surface designed to support their body correctly.

Without proper cat beds, your feline may default to sleeping on furniture, laundry baskets, or even cold floor surfaces, none of which provide the insulation, support, or psychological comfort that purpose-built cat beds deliver.

Whether your cat likes to curl up, stretch out, or hide away in a cosy cave, this guide will help you find cat beds that are safe, comfortable, and perfectly suited to their size, age, and sleeping style.

Scroll down to explore our top 5 best cat beds and shop by bed type to find the ideal match for your feline friend.

Top 5 Best Cat Beds This Year

These are our top picks for cosy, comfortable, and durable cat beds for all breeds and ages.

Donut Calming Cat Bed →

A plush, ultra-soft donut-shaped bed that helps anxious cats feel safe and secure.

  • Self-warming fleece fabric — retains your cat’s body heat naturally
  • Raised rim pillows — provide head and neck support
  • Machine washable — easy to clean and maintain
  • Anti-slip base — stays put on all floor types
  • Available in multiple sizes — fits kittens to large cats
View on Amazon →

Cat Cave Bed with Hood →

A fully enclosed cave-style bed that gives shy or nervous cats a cosy, private retreat.

  • Enclosed hood design — creates a secure, den-like space
  • Thick padded base — orthopaedic support for joints
  • Soft plush interior — ideal for cold UK winters
  • Lightweight and portable — move it room to room easily
  • Non-toxic materials — safe for all cats
View on Amazon →

Window Perch Cat Bed →

A suction-cup mounted window perch that gives indoor cats a sunny vantage point.

  • Strong suction cups — supports cats up to 15kg safely
  • Breathable mesh base — keeps cats cool in summer
  • Easy installation — no tools or drilling required
  • Perfect for bird watching — endless entertainment for indoor cats
  • Removable washable cover — hygienic and easy to maintain
View on Amazon →

Orthopaedic Memory Foam Cat Bed →

A supportive memory foam bed designed for senior cats and those with joint issues.

  • Memory foam filling — moulds to your cat’s body shape
  • Reduces joint pressure — ideal for arthritic or older cats
  • Waterproof liner — protects the foam from accidents
  • Removable zip cover — machine washable for easy care
  • Non-slip rubber base — stable on any surface
View on Amazon →

Heated Self-Warming Cat Bed →

A self-warming thermal bed that reflects your cat’s body heat without any electricity.

  • Self-warming thermal technology — no wires or electricity needed
  • Ultra-soft faux fur — cosy and inviting for cats
  • Perfect for senior cats — extra warmth soothes stiff joints
  • Foldable design — great for travel or crate use
  • Budget-friendly — effective warmth without running costs
View on Amazon →

Shop by Cat Bed Type

Find the perfect cat bed for your feline based on their personality, sleeping style, and comfort needs.

Donut & Bolster Beds →

Best for cats who love to curl up. Round, raised-edge designs create a cosy nest that makes cats feel safe and secure while resting their chin on the padded rim.

Cave & Hooded Beds →

Ideal for shy or nervous cats. Fully enclosed cave-style beds mimic den-like hiding spots cats seek in the wild, trapping body heat for extra warmth in winter.

Flat Mat & Cushion Beds →

Perfect for cats who stretch out. Simple padded surfaces ideal for windowsills, sofas, or cat trees. Affordable, easy to wash, and great for confident cats who prefer open sightlines.

Heated & Self-Warming Beds →

Great for senior cats and cold climates. Self-warming materials reflect body heat without electricity, while electric models offer thermostat-controlled warmth for arthritic cats.

Window Perch & Hammock Beds →

Best for indoor cats who love bird-watching. Suction-cup window perches and suspended hammocks give cats a sun-drenched, elevated vantage point for stimulation and relaxation.

Orthopaedic & Memory Foam Beds →

Essential for cats with arthritis or joint issues. Thick memory foam bases distribute weight evenly, relieving pressure points and providing superior spinal support for older cats.

How to Choose the Right Cat Bed

Choosing the right cat bed comes down to your cat’s age, size, sleeping style, and any joint or health issues. A well-chosen bed can improve their sleep quality, protect their joints, and keep them comfortable all year round.

📏 Size & Fit

Getting the right size is one of the most important steps. A cat bed that’s too small will be ignored, and one that’s too big may not feel cosy enough for cats that prefer snug spaces.

  • Measure your cat from nose to base of tail while lying stretched out, then add 20–25 cm.
  • For donut or cave beds, the interior diameter should match this measurement.
  • Large breeds like Maine Coons need XL beds — always check internal dimensions.
  • In multi-cat homes, provide one bed per cat plus one extra to prevent resource guarding.
  • Kittens need small, enclosed cat beds — they lose body heat quickly and crave security.
  • If your cat changes sleeping position often, choose a bed slightly larger than the stretched-out measurement to accommodate all postures.

🧵 Material & Fabric

The material affects warmth, durability, ease of cleaning, and how attractive the bed is to your cat. Different cats have strong tactile preferences.

  • Fleece & Sherpa: Soft, warm, affordable, and machine washable — a great all-rounder.
  • Plush & Velvet: Premium feel, attractive in modern interiors, irresistible for kneading cats.
  • Linen & Cotton: Breathable and ideal for warmer months or centrally heated homes.
  • Memory Foam: Exceptional joint support for senior cats — with removable washable covers.
  • Seasonal rotation: Many owners keep two beds — a breathable cotton one for summer and a thermal-lined one for winter.
  • Anxious cats: Soft, plush fabrics with raised edges or hoods help nervous cats feel safe and enclosed.

📍 Placement & Location

Even the most perfectly chosen bed will go unused if placed somewhere your cat finds unsuitable. Cats are particular about their sleeping environments.

  • Place beds in warm, quiet corners away from high-traffic areas and draughts.
  • Near radiators or sunny windowsills are consistently popular positions.
  • Many cats prefer elevated spots — on bookshelves, cat trees, or sturdy furniture.
  • In multi-storey homes, place a bed on each floor — especially for senior cats.
  • For shy or anxious cats, place the bed in a low-traffic corner or inside a closet with the door ajar — enclosed, quiet locations help them feel secure.
  • Observe your cat’s natural sleeping spots for a few days — then place the bed in their preferred location for the best chance of adoption.

🧹 Cleaning & Maintenance

Regular cleaning is essential for your cat’s health and the hygiene of your home. Unwashed beds accumulate fur, dander, and bacteria.

  • Choose beds with removable, machine-washable covers for easy maintenance.
  • Wash at 40°C every 2–3 weeks with a pet-safe detergent.
  • Shake outdoors and lint-roll before washing to prevent clogging the machine.
  • Replace beds when padding flattens or loses support — typically every 1–3 years.
  • For senior or arthritic cats, choose orthopaedic beds with waterproof liners — accidents become more common with age, and the liner protects expensive memory foam.
  • Rotating between two beds extends each bed’s lifespan and ensures your cat always has a clean option available.

Who This Page Is For

Choosing the right cat bed isn’t one-size-fits-all. This guide is designed to help every cat owner find the perfect fit based on their cat’s needs, habits, and sleeping style.

🐱 Senior & Arthritic Cats

If your cat needs extra joint support or struggles with stiffness, this guide highlights the best orthopaedic and memory foam beds for pain relief and comfort.

🐾 Kittens & Young Cats

For new kittens needing a cosy, secure space, Pet Hub Online covers enclosed cave beds, self-warming inserts, and washable options perfect for settling young cats into their new home.

🏠 Indoor Cats

Indoor cats need comfortable beds that provide warmth, stimulation, and a sense of territory. We cover window perches, hammocks, and elevated beds that keep indoor cats happy and rested.

Cat Beds FAQ

Why does my cat ignore their new cat bed?

This is one of the most common frustrations among cat owners. Cats are creatures of habit and strong olfactory preference, and a new cat bed that smells of the factory, packaging, or another animal may simply be unappealing at first. To encourage your cat to use new cat beds, place an item of your worn clothing inside or nearby to introduce a familiar scent. You can also sprinkle a small amount of catnip on or near the bed, or move the bed to a location you’ve observed your cat already gravitating towards. Give it at least two to three weeks before concluding your cat won’t use the bed — patience is key.

How many cat beds does my cat need?

The standard recommendation is one cat bed per cat, plus one extra. For a single-cat household, this means two cat beds — one primary sleeping spot and one alternative so the cat can choose based on their mood. In practice, many cats naturally gravitate towards three or four favourite sleeping spots around the home, so having cat beds available in each of those areas helps redirect them away from your furniture. In multi-cat homes, having more cat beds than cats is strongly advised to prevent resource competition.

Are heated cat beds safe?

When purchased from reputable brands and used correctly, heated cat beds are entirely safe. Self-warming cat beds that use reflective materials rather than electricity carry no electrical risk and are safe to leave out unsupervised. Electric heated cat beds should always bear a recognised UK safety mark and include automatic temperature regulation to prevent overheating. Never use a human heating pad as a substitute for proper heated cat beds, as these are not designed for animals and can cause serious burns. Always provide your cat with the option to move away from a heated bed if they become too warm.

Should I let my cat sleep in my bed or get them their own cat beds?

This is a personal choice, and both approaches are valid. Many cat owners enjoy the companionship of their cat sleeping alongside them, and research suggests this can strengthen the human-animal bond. However, having dedicated cat beds available gives your cat an alternative sleeping option and means you have a fallback if you prefer an undisturbed night. It’s also worth noting that cats are crepuscular and highly active in the early morning hours, which can disrupt human sleep. Providing comfortable cat beds in the bedroom — on a chair or at the foot of the bed — can satisfy your cat’s desire to be near you while giving you the option of boundaries.

How long do cat beds typically last?

The lifespan of cat beds varies considerably by quality and construction. Budget fleece cat beds that are washed frequently can begin to pill, lose shape, or lose their insulating properties within six to twelve months. Mid-range cat beds with quality construction and removable washable covers typically last two to four years. Premium orthopaedic cat beds with high-density foam can last five years or more, though the fabric cover may need replacing sooner. Signs that cat beds need replacing include flattened or lumpy padding that no longer provides support, permanent odours that washing cannot remove, or visible wear such as torn seams or exposed foam.

Can I make my own cat beds?

Absolutely. DIY cat beds are a popular option for crafty owners, and a simple sewn cushion cover filled with polyester stuffing or old fabric scraps can make a perfectly functional resting spot your cat may adore. Repurposed items such as old suitcases lined with a cushion, wooden crates with a mat insert, or cardboard boxes with fleece blankets all make creative and cost-effective cat beds. The main considerations are safety — avoid materials with loose threads, toxic dyes, or parts that could be ingested — and washability, as homemade cat beds still need to be regularly cleaned to stay hygienic.

Why Pet Owners Trust Pet Hub Online

🛡️

How to Clean and Maintain Cat Beds

Keeping your cat beds clean is essential for your cat’s health and your home’s hygiene. Most quality cat beds come with removable, machine-washable covers — look for this feature when buying cat beds, as it makes maintenance much easier.

Wash your cat beds at least once a fortnight, or weekly if you have multiple cats or outdoor cats. Use a pet-safe detergent and avoid fabric softeners, which can irritate sensitive skin. Between washes, vacuum your cat beds regularly to remove hair, dander, and dirt.

Inspect your cat beds monthly for signs of wear. Flat spots, lumpy filling, or worn-through fabric means it’s time for new cat beds. Most quality cat beds last 1–4 years depending on use. Rotating between two cat beds can extend their lifespan significantly.

5 Common Mistakes When Buying Cat Beds

The biggest mistake people make with cat beds is choosing the wrong size. Cat beds that are too small leave your cat cramped and uncomfortable, while oversized cat beds can make cats feel exposed and insecure. Always measure before buying cat beds.

Another common error is prioritising style over comfort when choosing cat beds. While it’s nice to have cat beds that match your décor, your cat’s comfort should always come first. Also, don’t place cat beds in high-traffic areas or near draughts — cats need a quiet, warm spot to rest. Ignoring your cat’s sleeping preferences is another mistake: observe whether they curl up, stretch out, or hide before choosing a bed style.

Seasonal Bedding Tips for Year-Round Comfort

Your cat’s sleeping needs change with the seasons. During summer months, breathable cotton mats, elevated perches, and mesh-based window beds help prevent overheating. Look for moisture-wicking fabrics and ventilated designs that promote airflow around your sleeping cat.

Winter calls for insulated, plush options with thermal linings or self-warming materials. Placing the sleeping area away from draughty windows and cold floors makes a significant difference to your cat’s comfort during colder months.

Regardless of season, the sleeping surface should always be appropriate for your cat’s weight and sleeping position. Side sleepers need wider, flatter surfaces, while cats that curl up prefer donut or nest-style options with supportive raised edges.

Waterproof liners are a worthwhile investment year-round. They protect the filling from accidents and damp fur. Most quality liners are machine-washable and add an extra layer of comfort while extending the life of your cat’s sleeping area.

Multi-cat households should provide each animal with their own dedicated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and stress. Position sleeping areas in separate quiet spots where each cat feels secure and undisturbed.

Placement Tips for Your Cat’s Sleeping Area

Where you position your cat’s resting spot matters as much as the product itself. Choose a quiet corner away from high-traffic doorways, draughts, and direct sunlight. A consistent location helps your cat associate that area with rest, making settling down at night or during the day far easier. If your home has multiple levels, consider placing a sleeping option on each floor so your cat always has a comfortable retreat nearby.

Temperature regulation plays a role in placement. During winter, move the sleeping area away from external walls and windows that radiate cold. In summer, tile or wood floors stay cooler than carpeted rooms, making them ideal locations. Window perches with mesh bases allow air circulation, which is particularly welcome for thick-coated breeds during warmer months.

For households with multiple cats, each animal should have their own designated resting space. Sharing can lead to resource guarding and disturbed sleep. Place sleeping areas within sight of each other if your cats are bonded, but far enough apart that each animal feels they have personal territory. This arrangement promotes harmony and ensures every companion gets the quality rest they need from their cat beds.

Matching the resting surface to your cat’s sleeping style improves comfort dramatically. Cats who curl up prefer donut or bolstered options that cradle their body. Cats who sprawl flat need a wide, unconstrained cushion. Side sleepers benefit from a low bolster on one edge that supports the neck without restricting movement. Observing how your cat naturally settles reveals which design will deliver the most restful and restorative sleep for their individual preference.

Why Dog Health & Care Matters

Your dog’s health is the foundation of a happy, active life together. From preventive care and nutrition to dental hygiene and parasite protection, the right products help you stay ahead of problems and keep your best friend thriving.

Shop by Type

Explore the essentials that support your dog’s health, comfort, and wellbeing at every life stage.

Supplements & Vitamins →

First Aid →

Eye & Ear Care →

Calming Products →
